title: TPU v1 · FEOL (Front-End-of-Line): building transistors step by step on the silicon surface—forming and doping wells, etching isolation trenches, growing gate oxide and forming gates, implanting source/drain regions, and creating contacts—turning the 65,536 8-bit MACs in the 256×256 systolic array, the CISC control logic, and every SRAM cell in the 24 MiB Unified Buffer/accumulators from bare silicon into switching transistors; transistor speed, leakage, and die-wide uniformity directly determine whether 700 MHz/92 TOPS is achievable and whether yield is acceptable, making FEOL the core competitive capability of the entire process
oneLine: This case focuses on “FEOL: building transistors.” Wafer fabrication is divided into front-end-of-line (FEOL) and back-end-of-line (BEOL); FEOL creates individual transistors (MOS switches) on the silicon surface. Following the sequence “well → isolation → gate (gate oxide + gate electrode) → source/drain → contact,” the process dopes the silicon, etches trenches, grows an ultrathin gate dielectric, forms the gate, implants the source/drain regions, and finally brings out contact terminals. For continuity, this page groups contact formation with FEOL; many foundry taxonomies classify contacts and local interconnect as middle-of-line (MOL), between FEOL transistor formation and BEOL global interconnect. The 65,536 8-bit MACs in TPU v1’s systolic array, the control logic that issues a dozen-plus CISC instructions, and every SRAM bit in the 24 MiB Unified Buffer and 4 MiB accumulator—occupying nearly 29% of the die—ultimately consist of vast numbers of such transistors, all formed during FEOL. Why is FEOL the process’s core competitive capability? Transistor speed (whether 700 MHz is attainable), leakage (whether power stays within 28–40 W), and die-wide uniformity (which governs the yield of a ≤331 mm² large die) are largely determined here. FinFET and GAA at advanced nodes likewise reshape the transistor in FEOL. This section uses TPU’s known architecture as the object being “implemented as transistors” to explain how FEOL is organized. The exact well, isolation, gate dielectric, source/drain, and contact processes; implant recipes; gate-dielectric thickness; strain/high-k metal-gate (HKMG) choices; and yield are standard foundry concerns for this class of 28 nm large chip, but TPU v1’s proprietary FEOL process and foundry are not public.

TPU v1 does not disclose its proprietary process/foundry. This diagram uses its known 256×256 array, control logic, and SRAM in the 24 MiB UB as the object being “implemented as transistors” to explain the concept</text></svg>

legend.name: ① Well (doping defines the foundation)
legend.desc: The first step in building transistors is to implant dopants into the silicon, defining N-wells and P-wells in the substrate. NMOS devices are built in P-wells and PMOS devices in N-wells, forming complementary CMOS. A well establishes the conductivity type and doping concentration beneath each transistor—the foundation for every switch—and its concentration directly affects threshold voltage and leakage. TPU’s 256×256 array, control logic, and SRAM are all built on this well structure. The exact implant dose and energy follow common 28 nm practice and are not disclosed for TPU.

legend.name: ② Isolation (STI trenches filled with oxide)
legend.desc: Individual transistors must be electrically isolated. Shallow trenches are etched between adjacent devices, filled with insulating oxide (shallow-trench isolation, STI), and planarized by CMP. Without isolation, neighboring transistors would leak, interfere with one another, or latch up, disrupting the entire array. TPU v1 packs 65,536 MACs, control logic, and large SRAM blocks into a ≤331 mm² die; at this density, isolation quality directly affects leakage and yield. STI details are standard foundry practice and are not disclosed for TPU.

legend.name: ③ Gate (gate dielectric + electrode, the switch’s critical control)
legend.desc: The heart of a transistor—and the most critical and demanding FEOL step—is first to grow an extremely thin gate dielectric on the silicon, then form the gate electrode above it (polysilicon, or a metal gate at advanced nodes). The gate is the switch’s control: applying voltage opens or closes the channel beneath it and controls current between source and drain. Gate-dielectric thickness and gate length (channel length) directly determine switching speed and leakage, making them fundamental to achieving 700 MHz/92 TOPS while meeting 28–40 W power. FinFET and GAA innovations transform this very structure. At 28 nm, high-k metal gate (HKMG) is common; TPU’s exact choice is not public.

legend.name: ④ Source/Drain (implanting the two terminals)
legend.desc: A further heavy implant is performed in the silicon on both sides of the gate to form the source and drain terminals. Current flows from source to drain through the silicon channel controlled by the gate. Source/drain doping, the junctions with the channel, and strain engineering used in modern processes to improve carrier mobility and transistor speed collectively determine drive strength. Every transistor in each TPU MAC and SRAM bit needs well-formed source/drain regions. The implant recipe follows common 28 nm practice; TPU’s proprietary values are not public.

legend.name: ⑤ Contact / MOL (bringing terminals up to BEOL)
legend.desc: At the FEOL-to-BEOL boundary, contact formation etches holes above the source, drain, and gate, then fills them with metal such as tungsten. This page groups the operation with FEOL for continuity, but many process taxonomies classify contacts and local interconnect as middle-of-line (MOL). This brings each transistor’s terminals from the silicon surface to the layers above, where BEOL uses a dozen-plus metal layers to connect them into the 256×256 array, control logic, and SRAM. Once contacts are complete, the individual transistors are in place but are not yet wired into circuits. FEOL builds the switches; BEOL wires them together; contacts form the handoff between the two. Contact-process details are standard foundry practice and are not disclosed for TPU.

steps.cap: ① Form wells: use ion implantation to create N-wells and P-wells in the silicon substrate, giving NMOS and PMOS devices the correct conductivity-type “foundation.” Well doping sets threshold voltage and leakage at their source.

② Create isolation: etch shallow trenches between devices, fill them with oxide (STI), and planarize the surface to isolate neighboring transistors and prevent leakage, crosstalk, and latch-up. At TPU’s device density, isolation integrity directly affects yield.

③ Form gates: grow an ultrathin gate dielectric on the silicon, then form the gate electrode (high-k metal gate is common at 28 nm). Gate-dielectric thickness and gate length decisively determine transistor speed and leakage, and therefore whether 700 MHz/92 TOPS and 28–40 W can be achieved. FinFET/GAA innovations target this structure.

④ Form source/drain regions: heavily implant the silicon on both sides of the gate to create source and drain terminals, leaving the gate-controlled channel between them. Modern processes also use strain to improve drive strength. Every transistor in every MAC and SRAM bit takes shape here.

⑤ Create contacts (often classified as MOL): etch contact holes above source, drain, and gate, fill them with metal, and bring the terminals upward for BEOL wiring. FEOL has already built the transistors for 65,536 MACs, the control logic, and all SRAM; this contact/MOL boundary exposes their terminals for BEOL.

before: Set transistor targets before defining the process: Before FEOL begins, the process must establish target transistor speed, threshold voltage, leakage, and drive strength from the design goals. TPU v1 must sustain a 256×256 array at 700 MHz while keeping power within 28–40 W, so FEOL must produce transistors that are fast enough without excessive leakage. This tradeoff must be fixed before well and gate formation.

Select the device architecture and material stack: Decide whether this layer uses planar MOS, FinFET, or GAA; which gate dielectric to use; whether the gate is polysilicon or high-k metal gate (HKMG); and whether strain engineering is needed. HKMG is common at 28 nm; advanced nodes change transistor structures—FinFET and GAA—within FEOL. TPU v1’s specific device and material choices are not public.

Define implant, gate-dielectric, and thermal-budget recipes: Well- and source/drain-implant dose and energy, gate-dielectric thickness, and the thermal budget for subsequent anneals must all be calibrated in advance. Exceeding the thermal budget can diffuse previously implanted dopants and shift transistor characteristics. These follow common 28 nm FEOL practice; TPU’s proprietary values are not public.

Align with upstream lithography/layout and the uniformity budget: Every FEOL step—well, isolation, gate, source/drain, and contact—depends on lithography to position its pattern, so it must align with the lithography and etch work in 3.2/3.3. A die-wide transistor-uniformity budget must also be set: the 256×256 array requires transistors across thousands of MACs to behave nearly identically, or the array cannot operate in lockstep.
during: FEOL in one sentence: build individual transistors (MOS switches) on the silicon surface. Wafer fabrication is divided into FEOL and BEOL: FEOL builds transistors; BEOL wires them. TPU v1’s 65,536 8-bit MACs, the control logic behind its dozen-plus CISC instructions, and every SRAM bit in the 24 MiB Unified Buffer and 4 MiB accumulator are all made from large numbers of transistors formed in this layer through a common sequence.

Five steps form a device, and their order is fixed: well → isolation → gate → source/drain → contact. First, implant dopants to define N/P wells (the foundation); then etch trenches, fill them with oxide, and isolate devices (STI); grow an ultrathin gate dielectric and form the gate (the switch control); heavily implant both sides of the gate to create source/drain regions (two terminals plus the channel); and finally etch contact holes over the terminals and fill them with metal (bringing connections upward). Contact and local-interconnect formation is often classified as MOL even though this teaching sequence groups it with FEOL. Each step combines “lithography to define the pattern” with implantation, deposition, or etching to realize it. The steps depend on one another; an incorrect order invalidates the structure.

The gate is decisive: gate-dielectric thickness and gate length determine performance and leakage. Applying voltage to the gate switches the underlying channel. A thinner gate dielectric and shorter gate length (channel) generally increase speed, but excessive scaling sharply raises leakage. TPU requires 700 MHz/92 TOPS performance while holding power to 28–40 W, placing this tension squarely on gate engineering. At 28 nm, high-k metal gate (HKMG) mitigates gate leakage; advanced nodes replace planar gates with FinFET/GAA structures to pursue both speed and low leakage.

Transistor quality = speed / leakage / uniformity; these three determine chip success. Insufficient speed means failing to reach 700 MHz; excessive leakage pushes power beyond 40 W and causes thermal problems; poor die-wide uniformity makes transistors across the 256×256 array differ, disrupting timing, reducing SRAM read/write margin, and potentially killing the die. FEOL’s core competitive capability is controlling all three simultaneously and consistently—one of the principal ways process technologies differentiate.

SRAM and logic share the same FEOL, but SRAM bits are especially sensitive. The 24 MiB UB and 4 MiB accumulator comprise vast SRAM arrays (6 transistors per bit) and occupy nearly 29% of the die. SRAM is highly sensitive to transistor matching and uniformity; even modest parameter variation can cause read/write errors. Achieving consistent transistors across such a large SRAM area is a central yield challenge for an AI chip whose on-chip memory occupies so much silicon.

Disclosure boundary (applies throughout): The exact well/STI/gate-dielectric/gate/source-drain/contact processes; ion-implant dose and energy; gate-dielectric thickness; strain and HKMG choices; anneal thermal budget; defects; and yield follow standard foundry FEOL practice for this class of 28 nm large chip/PCIe accelerator. TPU v1 does not disclose its proprietary FEOL process, device/material choices, foundry, or yield. This section uses only TPU’s known structure—the 256×256 array, CISC control logic, SRAM in the 24 MiB UB/4 MiB accumulator, 700 MHz, 28 nm, 28–40 W, and ≤331 mm²—as the object being “implemented as transistors” to explain FEOL concepts. The cross-section is illustrative and does not invent proprietary TPU manufacturing data.

result.label: FEOL output
result.value: Individual completed transistors (MOS switches) on the silicon surface: the transistors for TPU v1’s 65,536 MACs, CISC control logic, and all SRAM bits in the 24 MiB UB/4 MiB accumulator are formed here, although they are not yet connected into circuits

result.label: FEOL-to-MOL sequence
result.value: Well (doping defines the foundation) → STI isolation (trenches filled with oxide) → gate (gate dielectric + electrode, the switch control) → source/drain S/D (implanting two terminals + channel) → contact (bringing terminals upward; often classified as MOL)

result.label: Gate = critical control
result.value: Gate-dielectric thickness + gate length determine transistor speed and leakage; high-k metal gate (HKMG) is common at 28 nm; advanced-node FinFET/GAA changes target this very structure

result.label: Three transistor-quality metrics
result.value: Speed (whether 700 MHz/92 TOPS is attainable), leakage (whether 28–40 W is maintained), and uniformity (governing yield for the ≤331 mm² large die and the vast SRAM occupying nearly 29% of the die; SRAM uses 6 transistors per bit and is most sensitive to matching/uniformity)—FEOL’s core competitive capability

result.label: Handoff
result.value: After transistors and contacts are complete, BEOL uses a dozen-plus metal layers to connect them into the 256×256 array, control logic, and on-chip memory (FEOL builds switches; BEOL wires them)

result.label: Disclosure boundary
result.value: TPU v1 does not disclose its FEOL process, implant recipes, gate-dielectric thickness, HKMG choices, foundry, or yield; the material above reflects common foundry FEOL practice and illustrative structures, not proprietary TPU data

after: Measure the transistors to verify fabrication: After key FEOL steps, process-control test structures measure threshold voltage, drive current, leakage, gate-dielectric integrity, and other transistor parameters to confirm that they fall within target windows. Die-wide uniformity is also checked because the 256×256 array requires nearly identical behavior everywhere. Out-of-spec results must be traced back to the well, gate, or source/drain step responsible.

Only qualified devices proceed to BEOL wiring: Once transistors are complete, contacts are exposed, and parameters pass, the wafer enters BEOL. A dozen-plus metal layers connect the isolated switches into the systolic array, control logic, and SRAM. The switches built by FEOL become circuits only after BEOL wiring; no quality of interconnect can rescue a defective FEOL device.

Feed speed, leakage, and uniformity data back into the process: If transistors are too slow, too leaky, or nonuniform across the die, feed the measurements back to process engineering to tune implant dose, gate dielectric, anneal thermal budget, and related settings. Persistent shifts may require changes to device architecture or materials (planar ↔ HKMG ↔ FinFET). This feedback loop drives continuous FEOL yield and performance improvement.

Closely monitor SRAM and large-die uniformity: Give special attention to transistor matching across the vast SRAM occupying nearly 29% of the die and to parameter uniformity from the center to the edge of the ≤331 mm² large die. These are especially vulnerable to transistor variation, so they should be tracked, localized, and corrected separately as key yield risks.

Preserve an auditable record: Record transistor parameters, process-monitoring results, and uniformity data in the manufacturing process history for later fabrication, packaging/test, and yield-analysis review. This provides traceable evidence of FEOL manufacturing for each die; the specific data are proprietary to the foundry and are not public for TPU v1.
source: In-Datacenter Performance Analysis of a TPU, Jouppi et al., ISCA 2017 (the paper discloses TPU v1’s known structure—the 256×256 8-bit MAC systolic array = 65,536 MACs; control logic issuing a dozen-plus CISC instructions; SRAM in the 24 MiB Unified Buffer + 4 MiB accumulator; 700 MHz/92 TOPS; 28 nm; ≤331 mm²; and 28–40 W—which is used here as the object being “implemented as transistors”; the device-formation sequence spanning FEOL and the often-MOL contact boundary—well/STI isolation/gate dielectric + gate/source-drain/contact, ion implantation, gate processing, high-k metal gate (HKMG) and FinFET/GAA, the relationship from transistor speed/leakage/uniformity to yield, and gate-dielectric thickness, implant recipes, anneal thermal budgets, and related details reflect standard foundry/industry FEOL practice for this class of 28 nm large chip; the cross-section and terminals are illustrative and are not public disclosures of TPU v1’s foundry, proprietary process recipes, device/material choices, or yield)
